notes: This prototype was rebuilt from the CONSORT checklist and annotation guideline while preserving the existing prototype interface.
Counts are initialized to zero because Pass 1 does not use prediction errors or raw error logs.
Use this as the broad-coverage base; later passes can overwrite counts, cues, and boundary examples using gold examples and error logs.
metadata.construction_note: Preserves Pass 1 structure. Enriches section priors, cues, not-if rules, high-risk confusion links, and pairwise example slots using gold training labels. Error-count fields remain reserved for error-log Pass 3 and are not overwritten.
pass3_notes: Error-related counts and high-risk confusions were induced from raw error logs.
FP_ANNOTATION_UNDERLABEL and FP_VALID_ALTERNATIVE are counted separately and should not be treated as hard negative evidence.
Pairwise boundary examples are illustrative error cases, not gold guideline replacements.

must_have: The article title explicitly identifies the study as randomized/randomised or as a randomized/randomised trial.
not_if: The sentence is not the title or only says clinical trial without random allocation language.
The abstract or methods describe randomization but the title does not.
1a.definition: Identification as a randomised trial in the title

must_have: The first sentence or structured abstract text indicates a structured summary covering trial design, methods, results, and conclusions.
not_if: The sentence is a title, author line, keyword list, or abstract section header alone.
The sentence contains only background/objective content without abstract structure.
1b.definition: Structured summary of trial design, methods, results, and conclusions

must_have: Explains scientific background, clinical problem, prior evidence, evidence gap, or rationale for why the trial was undertaken.
not_if: The sentence only states the study objective/hypothesis; use 2b.
The sentence reports trial results or interpretation rather than pre-trial rationale.
Do not annotate more than the few best rationale sentences when the background spans many sentences.
The sentence states the specific objective/hypothesis without explaining why the trial was needed; use 2b.
2a.definition: Scientific background and explanation of rationale

must_have: States the specific objective, aim, purpose, research question, or hypothesis of the trial.
not_if: The sentence only gives broad disease background or rationale; use 2a.
The sentence defines outcome measures without an explicit objective/hypothesis; consider 6a.
The sentence only gives prior evidence or disease background without this trial objective; use 2a.

must_have: Describes the trial design type and/or allocation ratio, such as parallel, factorial, crossover, multicenter, superiority/noninferiority, blinded/placebo-controlled design.
not_if: The sentence only describes interventions without design structure; use 5.
The sentence only describes random sequence generation or concealment; use 8a/8b/9/10.
The sentence only states eligibility, intervention dose, or outcome definition without describing trial design.
The sentence says participants were randomized to receive a treatment dose/comparator; that is usually intervention details (5), not trial design (3a).
The sentence reports results or conclusions, even if it contains the word randomized.
3a.definition: Description of trial design (such as parallel, factorial) including allocation ratio

must_have: Describes important changes to trial methods after commencement, with a reason.
not_if: The sentence describes planned methods from the original protocol without a later change.
The sentence reports why the trial ended/stopped; use 14b.
The sentence describes planned design at trial start rather than a change after commencement.
3b.definition: Important changes to methods after trial commencement (such as eligibility criteria), with reasons

must_have: States eligibility, inclusion, or exclusion criteria for participants.
not_if: The sentence only states setting/location; use 4b.
The sentence only describes baseline characteristics of enrolled participants; use 15.
The sentence describes study location/setting rather than who was eligible; use 4b.
Do not label broad population summaries or registry-subgroup descriptions as eligibility criteria unless inclusion/exclusion criteria are explicit.

must_have: States settings, locations, sites, clinics, hospitals, countries, centers, or time/place where data were collected.
not_if: The sentence only gives participant eligibility criteria; use 4a.
The sentence only gives recruitment/follow-up dates; use 14a.
The sentence describes eligibility criteria rather than study setting/location; use 4a.
Do not label equipment, scanners, coils, assays, or measurement platforms as settings/locations when they define outcome assessment; consider 6a.
4b.definition: Settings and locations where the data were collected

must_have: Describes what each group received with enough intervention/comparator details for replication, including dose, timing, delivery, duration, or administration rules.
not_if: The sentence describes outcome assessment or measurement procedure rather than treatment; use 6a.
The sentence only describes design/allocation ratio; use 3a.
5.definition: The interventions for each group with sufficient details to allow replication, including how and when they were actually administered

must_have: Defines pre-specified primary or secondary outcome measures/endpoints, including what was assessed and, when available, how and when assessed.
Measurement apparatus, imaging protocols, laboratory assays, marker definitions, timing of assessment, and quantification procedures can satisfy 6a when they define how outcomes were assessed.
not_if: The sentence describes the statistical comparison method; use 12a.
The sentence reports outcome results; use 17a/17b.
The sentence describes additional/exploratory measures rather than primary/secondary outcomes unless they are explicitly trial outcomes.
The sentence describes a future or external trial rather than outcome measures in the current trial.
6a.definition: Completely defined pre-specified primary and secondary outcome measures, including how and when they were assessed

must_have: Describes changes to trial outcomes/endpoints after trial commencement, with reasons.
not_if: The sentence describes original outcomes only; use 6a.
The sentence describes other method changes; use 3b.
6b.definition: Any changes to trial outcomes after the trial commenced, with reasons

must_have: Explains how sample size was determined, including assumptions such as effect size, power, alpha, dropout, or target enrollment.
not_if: The sentence only reports actual enrollment/randomized numbers; use 13a.
The sentence only describes statistical analysis methods; use 12a.

must_have: Explains interim analyses, stopping guidelines, stopping boundaries, or monitoring rules when applicable.
not_if: The sentence reports actual trial stop/end reason; use 14b.
The sentence describes final statistical analysis without interim/stopping plan; use 12a.
7b.definition: When applicable, explanation of any interim analyses and stopping guidelines

must_have: Describes the method used to generate the random allocation sequence.
not_if: The sentence only describes blocking/stratification/restriction; use 8b.
The sentence describes allocation concealment mechanism; use 9.
The sentence says who generated/enrolled/assigned; use 10.
The sentence only describes blocking/stratification/restriction without how the sequence was generated; use 8b.
8a.definition: Method used to generate the random allocation sequence

must_have: Describes type of randomization and restrictions such as blocking, block size, stratification, minimization, or allocation ratio.
not_if: The sentence only says the sequence was computer-generated; use 8a.
The sentence describes concealment or implementation roles; use 9/10.
The sentence only says computer-generated/random list without restriction details; use 8a.
8b.definition: Type of randomisation; details of any restriction (such as blocking and block size)

must_have: Describes the allocation concealment mechanism and steps that hid the sequence until assignment.
not_if: The sentence only says who generated/enrolled/assigned participants; use 10.
The sentence only describes sequence generation; use 8a/8b.
The sentence identifies who generated/enrolled/assigned without concealment mechanism; use 10.
9.definition: Mechanism used to implement the random allocation sequence, describing any steps taken to conceal the sequence until interventions were assigned

must_have: States who generated the allocation sequence, who enrolled participants, and/or who assigned participants to interventions.
not_if: The sentence only describes concealment mechanism without human/role implementation details; use 9.
The sentence only describes sequence generation method; use 8a.
The sentence only describes concealment mechanism such as sealed envelopes or central concealment; use 9.
10.definition: Who generated the random allocation sequence, who enrolled participants, and who assigned participants to interventions

must_have: States who was blinded/masked after assignment and/or how blinding was maintained.
not_if: The sentence only says interventions looked similar; use 11b.
The sentence describes allocation concealment before assignment; use 9.
The sentence only says placebo/intervention looked similar; use 11b.

Blinded event adjudication or blinded image analysis is not necessarily full CONSORT blinding of participants, care providers, or outcome assessors unless the blinded role and post-assignment context are explicit.
11a.definition: If done, who was blinded after assignment to interventions and how

must_have: Describes similarity of interventions/comparators relevant to blinding, such as matching placebo appearance, taste, color, packaging, or content.
not_if: The sentence only states who was blinded; use 11a.
The sentence describes the active intervention dose or schedule without similarity/blinding; use 5.
The sentence only names who was blinded; use 11a.
11b.definition: If relevant, description of the similarity of interventions

must_have: Describes statistical methods used to compare groups for primary or secondary outcomes.
Statistical software, significance thresholds, transformations, normality checks, multiplicity corrections, and confidence-limit reporting can all be valid statistical-method evidence.
not_if: The sentence defines outcomes/endpoints without statistical method; use 6a.
The sentence describes methods for subgroup/adjusted/additional analyses specifically; use 12b.
The sentence reports results of analyses; use 17a/18.
12a.definition: Statistical methods used to compare groups for primary and secondary outcomes

must_have: Describes statistical methods for additional, subgroup, adjusted, sensitivity, exploratory, or ancillary analyses.
not_if: The sentence describes the main statistical method for primary/secondary outcomes; use 12a.
The sentence reports results of additional analyses; use 18.
12b.definition: Methods for additional analyses, such as subgroup analyses and adjusted analyses

must_have: Reports participant flow counts by group: randomly assigned, received intended treatment, and/or analyzed for the primary outcome.
not_if: The sentence reports losses/exclusions and reasons after randomization; use 13b.
The sentence reports baseline characteristics; use 15.
The sentence reports denominators for each analysis population; use 16 if focused on analysis denominator.
The sentence focuses on losses/exclusions and reasons after randomization; use 13b.
13a.definition: For each group, the numbers of participants who were randomly assigned, received intended treatment, and were analysed for the primary outcome

must_have: Reports losses and exclusions after randomization with reasons.
Short standalone sentences giving the reason for discontinuation, withdrawal, loss, exclusion, or protocol deviation are valid 13b evidence.
not_if: The sentence only reports flow counts without reasons; use 13a.
The sentence reports harms as trial outcomes rather than reasons for discontinuation; use 19.
The sentence reports adverse event rates as harms rather than participant-flow reasons; use 19.
13b.definition: For each group, losses and exclusions after randomisation, together with reasons

must_have: States dates or periods of recruitment and/or follow-up.
not_if: The sentence only states setting/location without dates; use 4b.
The sentence explains why the trial stopped; use 14b.
The sentence explains why the trial stopped or ended early; use 14b.
14a.definition: Dates defining the periods of recruitment and follow-up

must_have: Explains why the trial ended or was stopped, including early termination or external stopping reason.
not_if: The sentence only gives planned recruitment/follow-up dates; use 14a.
The sentence describes interim stopping guidelines rather than actual ending reason; use 7b.
The sentence only gives recruitment/follow-up dates without explaining trial stopping; use 14a.
14b.definition: Why the trial ended or was stopped

must_have: Reports or points to baseline demographic and clinical characteristics for each group.
Table or figure headings such as “Table 1 Baseline Characteristics” are valid evidence when they identify baseline characteristic reporting.
not_if: The sentence only reports randomization/flow counts; use 13a.
The sentence reports post-treatment outcome results; use 17a/18.
15.definition: A table showing baseline demographic and clinical characteristics for each group

must_have: Reports the number of participants included in each analysis and/or whether analyses followed original assigned groups such as intention-to-treat.
not_if: The sentence only reports randomized/received/analyzed flow counts; use 13a.
The sentence describes statistical method without denominator/analysis population; use 12a.
The sentence reports participant flow counts without analysis denominator or analysis population; use 13a.
16.definition: For each group, number of participants included in each analysis and whether the analysis was by original assigned groups

must_have: Reports results for primary or secondary outcomes by group and/or effect estimates with precision.
not_if: The sentence reports additional/subgroup/adjusted/exploratory analysis results; use 18.
The sentence reports harms; use 19.
The sentence interprets results in Discussion rather than reporting Results-section estimates; use 22.
For binary outcomes with both absolute and relative effects, consider 17b.
The sentence is in Discussion/Abstract and only summarizes or interprets results without original Results-section estimates.
The sentence reports a p-value or qualitative significance without an effect estimate or precision when the annotation requires full outcome reporting.
The sentence reports exploratory/biomarker/subgroup/additional analyses rather than primary/secondary outcome results; consider 18.
17a.definition: For each primary and secondary outcome, results for each group, and the estimated effect size and its precision

must_have: For a binary outcome, presents both absolute and relative effect sizes or enough paired absolute and relative reporting.
not_if: The outcome is continuous or ordinal without binary event/proportion interpretation.
The sentence gives only a percentage/proportion or only a risk ratio/odds ratio; use 17a.
Harms binary outcomes usually belong to 19 unless clearly primary/secondary outcome reporting.
17b.definition: For binary outcomes, presentation of both absolute and relative effect sizes is recommended

must_have: Reports results of other analyses, including subgroup, adjusted, ancillary, exploratory, post hoc, sensitivity, or additional analyses, while distinguishing pre-specified from exploratory when stated.
Treat biomarker, correlation, subgroup, adjusted, exploratory, ancillary, lipid/inflammation marker result sentences as 18 when they are not clearly the main primary/secondary outcome results.
not_if: The sentence reports main primary/secondary outcome results; use 17a.
The sentence describes methods for additional analyses; use 12b.
The sentence interprets additional findings in Discussion; use 22.
18.definition: Results of any other analyses performed, including subgroup analyses and adjusted analyses, distinguishing pre-specified from exploratory

must_have: Reports important harms, adverse events, unintended effects, safety outcomes, or adverse event counts/rates by group.
not_if: The sentence explains participant losses/exclusions due to adverse events; use 13b if the function is participant flow.
The sentence interprets safety in Discussion; consider 22.
The sentence defines safety monitoring outcome in Methods; use 6a.
19.definition: All important harms or unintended effects in each group

must_have: Reports a specific trial limitation, potential bias, imprecision, multiplicity issue, limited power, missing measure, or design/data constraint.
Specific inability to evaluate safety, dose adequacy, plaque features, selection, measurement, follow-up, or sample-size limitations should be treated as 20.
not_if: The sentence merely says the study has limitations without naming one.
The sentence gives interpretation or implications without a concrete limitation; use 22.
The sentence is about generalizability/applicability; use 21.
20.definition: Trial limitations, addressing sources of potential bias, imprecision, and multiplicity of analyses

must_have: Discusses generalizability, external validity, or applicability of trial findings to other populations, settings, patients, or practice contexts.
not_if: The sentence only interprets clinical meaning without applicability/external-validity framing; use 22.
The sentence only names a limitation; use 20.

must_have: Interprets trial findings consistently with results, balances benefits and harms, compares with other evidence, or states clinical/scientific implications.
not_if: The sentence only reports raw Results-section estimates; use 17a/18/19.
The sentence only states a specific limitation; use 20.
The sentence only addresses external validity/generalizability; use 21.
Summary-box statements should usually not be annotated unless the item appears nowhere else.
22.definition: Interpretation consistent with results, balancing benefits and harms, and considering other relevant evidence

must_have: States the registration number and name of the trial registry.
not_if: The sentence only says the study was approved by an ethics committee or gives protocol access; use 24 for protocol access.
The sentence cites a registry without registration number when no registry identification is given.
23.definition: Registration number and name of trial registry

must_have: States where the full trial protocol can be accessed.
not_if: The sentence only gives trial registration number/name; use 23.
The sentence only describes protocol approval/amendment without access location.
24.definition: Where the full trial protocol can be accessed, if available

must_have: States sources of funding/support, supply of drugs/materials, sponsor support, and/or role of funders.
not_if: The sentence only reports conflicts of interest, authorship contribution, ethics approval, or acknowledgements without funding/support or funder role.
The sentence only describes registration/protocol access; use 23/24.
25.definition: Sources of funding and other support, role of funders

17a__18.rule: Use 17a for main primary/secondary outcome results in the Results section; use 18 for additional, subgroup, adjusted, exploratory, ancillary, biomarker, lipid/inflammation, or correlation analyses.
17a__18.choose_first_when: The sentence reports a pre-specified primary/secondary endpoint or main Results-section comparison for the trial outcomes.
17a__18.choose_second_when: The sentence reports exploratory/additional biomarkers, lipid/inflammation markers, subgroup/correlation/adjusted analyses, or other analyses beyond primary/secondary outcomes.
17a__18.do_not: Do not assign 17a to biomarker or exploratory result sentences just because they include p-values or confidence intervals.

global_rules: If a sentence does not fit neatly into any CONSORT item, do not annotate it.
If automatic sentence splitting divides one relevant sentence into multiple fragments, annotate all relevant fragments with the applicable item.
Do not annotate subsection titles alone; prefer sentences with actual checklist content.
Use the typical CONSORT section as a strong prior, but allow cross-section annotation when the item appears only outside its usual section.
Other information items 23, 24, and 25 may be annotated in any section when explicitly stated.
In abstracts, annotate 1b when appropriate; additionally annotate items mentioned only in the abstract, such as trial registration.
For table or figure evidence, annotate the caption when available; otherwise annotate the narrative sentence that refers to the table or figure.
Avoid annotating caption sentences that only explain symbols, abbreviations, or formatting and do not report checklist content.
Summary boxes such as “What this study adds” should usually not be annotated unless they contain an item not addressed elsewhere.
For anaphoric interpretation, annotate the sentence that carries the checklist meaning; do not over-annotate antecedent sentences solely to resolve pronouns.
For outcome-related items, first decide whether the sentence concerns primary/secondary outcomes or additional analyses; this boundary affects 6a/6b, 12a/12b, 17a/17b, and 18.
For 17a vs 17b, determine whether the outcome is binary and whether both absolute and relative effect sizes are presented.
For item 20, annotate only specific limitations; do not annotate generic statements that merely say the study has limitations.
When a sentence explicitly satisfies multiple item definitions, preserve multiple labels rather than forcing a single label prematurely.
Pass 2 gold data confirms strong section priors: Methods for items 3–12, Results for items 13–19, Discussion for items 20–22, and flexible sections for administrative items 23–25.

Gold examples include table and figure captions as valid evidence when captions directly identify baseline characteristics, participant disposition, efficacy outcomes, blood measures, or adverse events.
Do not annotate bare section headers such as “Patients”, “Methods”, or “Study design” unless the text itself contains checklist-relevant content.

When the training examples annotate table titles such as baseline characteristics, subject disposition, or efficacy outcomes, treat the caption/title as evidence for the corresponding item rather than discarding it as too short.

Use outcome hierarchy explicitly: define outcomes in 6a/6b, analyze primary/secondary outcomes in 12a, analyze additional/subgroup/adjusted outcomes in 12b, report primary/secondary results in 17a/17b, and report other analyses in 18.
Treat FP_ANNOTATION_UNDERLABEL and FP_VALID_ALTERNATIVE as soft evidence of possible annotation incompleteness, not as hard negative rules.
For Results items, prefer Results-section evidence over Discussion/Abstract restatements unless the target item is only available outside the typical section.
For 17a, do not accept qualitative result summaries without sufficient estimate/precision when a stricter annotated Results sentence is available.
For 6a, measurement instruments, laboratory assays, imaging protocols, and quantification procedures are valid outcome-assessment evidence when tied to trial outcomes.
Standalone table and figure headings can be valid evidence when they name baseline characteristics, outcome results, additional analyses, harms, or participant flow.
